name: Anime & Manga
description: From Astro Boy to Demon Slayer — test your knowledge of anime, manga, their legendary creators, and the culture that conquered the world.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mangaka created Dragon Ball?
facts.explanation: Akira Toriyama (1955–2024) created Dragon Ball, which was serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump from 1984 to 1995. The series became one of the best-selling manga franchises of all time, with over 260 million copies sold worldwide. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which manga holds the Guinness World Record as the best-selling manga series of all time, with over 500 million copies sold?
facts.explanation: One Piece by Eiichiro Oda has sold over 500 million copies worldwide, earning a Guinness World Record as the best-selling manga series ever. It has been serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump since 1997. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mangaka created Naruto?
facts.explanation: Masashi Kishimoto created Naruto, serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump from 1999 to 2014 across 700 chapters and 72 volumes. Over 250 million copies of the manga have been sold worldwide. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Dragon Ball Z first air in Japan?
facts.explanation: Dragon Ball Z premiered in Japan in 1989 as a direct continuation of the original Dragon Ball anime. It ran until 1996 and became one of the most influential anime series in Western markets. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mangaka created One Piece?
facts.explanation: Eiichiro Oda created One Piece, which has been serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump since 1997. The series has broken multiple sales records and Oda is one of the best-selling fiction authors of all time. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which 2020 anime film earned $504 million and became the highest-grossing Japanese film ever at the time of its release?
facts.explanation: Demon Slayer: Mugen Train (2020), produced by ufotable, grossed approximately $504 million worldwide and surpassed Spirited Away to become the highest-grossing Japanese film and highest-grossing anime film in history. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Who is the protagonist of the Naruto manga and anime series?
facts.explanation: Naruto Uzumaki is the title protagonist of Masashi Kishimoto's Naruto series. A young ninja who dreams of becoming Hokage, he is best known for his signature move the Shadow Clone Jutsu (Kage Bunshin no Jutsu). Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did One Piece begin its serialization in Weekly Shonen Jump?
facts.explanation: One Piece began serialization in Weekly Shonen Jump on July 22, 1997, and continues to this day, making it one of the longest-running manga series ever. Creator Eiichiro Oda has maintained the series for nearly three decades. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which anime studio produced both Fullmetal Alchemist (2003) and Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood (2009)?
facts.explanation: Studio Bones produced both anime adaptations of Hiromu Arakawa's Fullmetal Alchemist. Brotherhood (2009, 64 episodes) is considered the definitive adaptation as it faithfully follows the manga's story. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mangaka created Bleach?
facts.explanation: Tite Kubo created Bleach, which ran in Weekly Shonen Jump from 2001 to 2016 across 686 chapters. The series is part of the 'Big Three' — the trio of dominant WSJ series alongside Naruto and One Piece. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: How many episodes did Naruto Shippuden run for?
facts.explanation: Naruto Shippuden ran for 500 episodes, airing from 2007 to 2017. It was produced by Studio Pierrot and adapted the second half of Masashi Kishimoto's manga, covering Naruto's teenage years and the Fourth Shinobi World War. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio has produced the Dragon Ball anime franchise since 1986?
facts.explanation: Toei Animation has produced every main Dragon Ball anime series since 1986, including Dragon Ball, Dragon Ball Z, Dragon Ball Super, and Dragon Ball GT. The studio is one of Japan's oldest and most prolific animation houses. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Who is the protagonist of My Hero Academia, also known by his hero name 'Deku'?
facts.explanation: Izuku Midoriya, nicknamed Deku, is the protagonist of Kohei Horikoshi's My Hero Academia. Born without a Quirk in a world where 80% of people have superpowers, he inherits the legendary ability One For All from the Symbol of Peace, All Might. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mangaka created Fullmetal Alchemist?
facts.explanation: Hiromu Arakawa created Fullmetal Alchemist, serialized in Monthly Shonen Gangan from 2001 to 2010. Arakawa is one of the most celebrated female mangaka in the shonen genre, which is traditionally male-dominated. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Along with Naruto and One Piece, which series completed the 'Big Three' dominant Weekly Shonen Jump titles of the 2000s?
facts.explanation: Bleach, Naruto, and One Piece were collectively known as the 'Big Three' — the three simultaneously running Weekly Shonen Jump titles that dominated global anime and manga popularity throughout the 2000s. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mangaka created Hunter x Hunter — and is married to Sailor Moon creator Naoko Takeuchi?
facts.explanation: Yoshihiro Togashi, who also created Yu Yu Hakusho, wrote Hunter x Hunter and is married to Naoko Takeuchi, creator of Sailor Moon. Togashi is notorious for extended hiatuses due to health issues, making new chapters major events. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mangaka created Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba?
facts.explanation: Koyoharu Gotouge created Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba, serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump from 2016 to 2020 across 205 chapters. The series became a cultural phenomenon, largely fueled by ufotable's highly acclaimed anime adaptation. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: How many chapters did the original Naruto manga run for, from 1999 to 2014?
facts.explanation: The Naruto manga ran for exactly 700 chapters, serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump from 1999 to 2014. Masashi Kishimoto later continued the story via Boruto: Naruto Next Generations. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Who is the protagonist of Jujutsu Kaisen, a high school student who swallows a cursed finger?
facts.explanation: Yuji Itadori is the protagonist of Gege Akutami's Jujutsu Kaisen. After swallowing a cursed finger belonging to the King of Curses Ryomen Sukuna, he becomes Sukuna's vessel and enrolls in Jujutsu High to hunt down the remaining fingers. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produced the Jujutsu Kaisen anime series?
facts.explanation: MAPPA produced the Jujutsu Kaisen anime, which premiered in 2020. The studio is known for high-quality action animation and also produced Attack on Titan's final season. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produced Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba, known for its breathtaking visual effects?
facts.explanation: ufotable produced Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba, earning widespread praise for its fluid animation and cinematic visual effects, particularly the 'Total Concentration Breathing' battle sequences. Their work contributed significantly to the anime's record-breaking film performance. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Who is the rubber-bodied protagonist of One Piece, seeking to become King of the Pirates?
facts.explanation: Monkey D. Luffy is the protagonist of Eiichiro Oda's One Piece. After eating a Devil Fruit, his body gained rubber-like properties. His goal is to find the legendary treasure called the One Piece and become King of the Pirates. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produced the Naruto and Naruto Shippuden anime series?
facts.explanation: Studio Pierrot produced both Naruto (220 episodes, 2002–2007) and Naruto Shippuden (500 episodes, 2007–2017). Pierrot is also known for producing Bleach and Tokyo Ghoul. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which manga series by Akira Toriyama was serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump from 1984 to 1995, running 519 chapters?
facts.explanation: Dragon Ball was serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump from November 1984 to May 1995, spanning 519 chapters compiled into 42 tankōbon volumes. It popularized the power-up trope and transformed shonen manga globally. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Yoshihiro Togashi series features a power system called 'Nen' and a protagonist named Gon Freecss?
facts.explanation: Hunter x Hunter features Nen, a system of manipulating one's life energy into six types for combat and special abilities. Protagonist Gon Freecss is a young boy searching for his father, the legendary Hunter Ging Freecss. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood premiere, the anime adaptation that faithfully follows the manga?
facts.explanation: Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood premiered in 2009, produced by Studio Bones. Unlike the 2003 adaptation which diverged from the unfinished manga, Brotherhood aired after the manga concluded and faithfully adapted Hiromu Arakawa's complete story. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produces the My Hero Academia anime series?
facts.explanation: Studio Bones has produced My Hero Academia since the series began airing in 2016. Bones is known for high-quality action animation and also produced both Fullmetal Alchemist anime adaptations. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: How many episodes did the original Bleach anime run for before its first cancellation in 2012?
facts.explanation: The original Bleach anime ran for 366 episodes from 2004 to 2012, produced by Studio Pierrot. The series was later revived in 2022 with Bleach: Thousand-Year Blood War, which adapts the manga's final arc. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Akira Toriyama manga franchise has sold over 260 million copies worldwide and popularized the power-up trope globally?
facts.explanation: Dragon Ball has sold over 260 million manga copies worldwide, making it one of the best-selling manga franchises in history. Its influence on action manga and anime is immeasurable — concepts like power levels and transformation sequences trace directly to Toriyama's work. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Who is the orange-haired substitute Soul Reaper protagonist of Bleach?
facts.explanation: Ichigo Kurosaki is the protagonist of Tite Kubo's Bleach. A teenager with unusual spiritual awareness, he gains Soul Reaper powers and uses a zanpakuto (soul-cutting sword) to defend the living world from evil spirits called Hollows. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Kohei Horikoshi series is set in a world where 80% of the population has superpowers called 'Quirks'?
facts.explanation: My Hero Academia is set in a world where the majority of humanity has developed superhuman abilities called Quirks. The story follows Izuku Midoriya, born without a Quirk, who inherits the power of the greatest hero and trains to become a professional hero himself. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which white-haired Jujutsu Kaisen teacher, who wears a blindfold, became one of the most popular anime characters of the 2020s?
facts.explanation: Satoru Gojo is the most powerful jujutsu sorcerer in Gege Akutami's Jujutsu Kaisen universe. His charismatic personality and enormous power — including the Limitless technique and Six Eyes — made him a global fan favorite almost immediately after the anime's 2020 premiere. Source: Wikipedia.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the manga series Sailor Moon, serialized in Nakayoshi magazine from 1991 to 1997?
facts.explanation: Naoko Takeuchi created Sailor Moon, which ran in Kodansha's Nakayoshi magazine from 1991 to 1997. The series defined and popularized the magical girl genre worldwide and has sold over 70 million manga copies globally.

facts.quizQuestion: How many episodes did the original Sailor Moon anime by Toei Animation run from 1992 to 1997?
facts.explanation: The original Sailor Moon anime produced by Toei Animation aired from 1992 to 1997 and ran for 200 episodes across five seasons. A new anime adaptation, Sailor Moon Crystal, began streaming in 2014 with a modernized art style closer to the original manga.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the clumsy teenage girl who transforms into the titular heroine in Sailor Moon?
facts.explanation: Usagi Tsukino is the protagonist of Sailor Moon, a 14-year-old middle school student who discovers she is a magical warrior destined to protect Earth. Her name translates roughly to 'Rabbit of the Moon,' referencing the Japanese folk tale of a rabbit living on the moon.

facts.quizQuestion: Which all-female manga collective created Cardcaptor Sakura, serialized in Nakayoshi from 1996 to 2000?
facts.explanation: CLAMP, a four-woman manga collective, created Cardcaptor Sakura, which ran in Kodansha's Nakayoshi magazine from 1996 to 2000. The group is known for elaborate character designs and has also produced X/1999, Magic Knight Rayearth, and xxxHolic.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produced the original Cardcaptor Sakura anime series that aired in 1998?
facts.explanation: Madhouse produced the Cardcaptor Sakura anime, which aired from 1998 to 2000 for 70 episodes. Madhouse is also known for producing Death Note, Monster, and Hunter x Hunter (2011), making it one of the most versatile anime studios of its era.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many copies of Fruits Basket manga had been sold, making it one of the best-selling shojo series ever?
facts.explanation: Fruits Basket by Natsuki Takaya sold over 30 million copies, cementing its place as one of the best-selling shojo manga of all time. Serialized in Hana to Yume magazine from 1998 to 2006, the series was given a faithful full-length anime adaptation by TMS Entertainment from 2019 to 2021.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio produced the 2019 Fruits Basket anime remake that faithfully adapted the full manga for the first time?
facts.explanation: TMS Entertainment produced the 2019 to 2021 remake of Fruits Basket, which ran for 63 episodes across three seasons. Unlike the original 2001 anime that ended before the manga concluded, this adaptation covered the entire story including its dramatic final arc.

facts.quizQuestion: The manga Nana by Ai Yazawa, featuring two protagonists sharing the same first name, targets which demographic?
facts.explanation: Nana is classified as josei manga, targeting adult women, and was serialized in Cookie magazine (Shueisha). While often confused with shojo due to its emotional story, its mature themes of careers, romance, and self-identity place it firmly in the josei category.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many million copies of Nana manga had been sold before its serialization went on indefinite hiatus in 2009?
facts.explanation: Nana by Ai Yazawa sold approximately 50 million copies before the series went on hiatus in 2009 due to Yazawa's illness. The manga ran in Cookie magazine from 2000 to 2009 and was adapted into a 47-episode anime by Madhouse in 2006.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produced the 2006 Ouran High School Host Club anime, known for its gender-bending humor?
facts.explanation: Bones, the studio behind Fullmetal Alchemist and Cowboy Bebop: Knockin' on Heaven's Door, produced the 26-episode Ouran High School Host Club anime in 2006. Based on Bisco Hatori's manga from LaLa magazine, the series became a landmark reverse harem comedy.

facts.quizQuestion: Which former Sailor Moon director created Revolutionary Girl Utena, the 1997 anime celebrated for its feminist symbolism?
facts.explanation: Kunihiko Ikuhara directed Revolutionary Girl Utena after leaving Toei Animation, where he had been a key director on Sailor Moon. The 39-episode series aired in 1997 and is acclaimed for its surrealist imagery, deconstruction of fairy tale tropes, and exploration of gender and power.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio produced the 2018 anime adaptation of Banana Fish, Akimi Yoshida's thriller manga set in New York City?
facts.explanation: MAPPA produced the 24-episode Banana Fish anime in 2018, adapting Akimi Yoshida's manga that ran in Shojo Comic from 1985 to 1994. The series was notable for updating its setting from the 1980s to the present day while preserving the story's exploration of crime, trauma, and friendship.

facts.quizQuestion: In which Shogakukan shojo magazine was Banana Fish serialized from 1985 to 1994?
facts.explanation: Banana Fish by Akimi Yoshida ran in Shogakukan's Shojo Comic magazine from 1985 to 1994. Despite its shojo label, the series broke conventions with its gritty crime thriller content set in New York City, helping expand what topics shojo manga could tackle.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the dark fantasy manga Berserk, serialized in Young Animal magazine since 1989?
facts.explanation: Kentaro Miura (1966–2021) created Berserk, one of the most celebrated dark fantasy manga ever published. After Miura's passing in 2021, the series was continued by his assistants and close friend Kouji Mori using Miura's notes. Berserk has sold approximately 60 million copies worldwide.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the black-swordsman protagonist of Berserk, a former mercenary haunted by supernatural enemies?
facts.explanation: Guts is the protagonist of Berserk, a lone mercenary known as the Black Swordsman who wields an enormous sword called the Dragonslayer. His story is defined by his time with the Band of the Hawk and the traumatic betrayal that left him cursed to battle demonic forces.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the landmark Ghost in the Shell cyberpunk film by Mamoru Oshii and Production I.G released?
facts.explanation: The Ghost in the Shell film directed by Mamoru Oshii was released in 1995 and is widely considered one of the most influential works in anime and science fiction. The film's philosophical exploration of identity and consciousness in a cybernetic world is cited as a direct inspiration for The Matrix (1999).

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the cyborg public-security officer who serves as the protagonist of Ghost in the Shell?
facts.explanation: Major Motoko Kusanagi is the protagonist of Ghost in the Shell, a highly cybernetically enhanced officer in Section 9, a covert government anti-crime unit. Her philosophical questioning of her own humanity and identity forms the thematic core of both the manga by Masamune Shirow and the 1995 film.

facts.quizQuestion: How many episodes did the Monster anime by Madhouse run when it adapted Naoki Urasawa's psychological thriller manga?
facts.explanation: The Monster anime by Madhouse ran for 74 episodes from 2004 to 2005, faithfully adapting Naoki Urasawa's manga that ran in Big Comic Original from 1994 to 2001. The series follows Dr. Kenzo Tenma, a Japanese surgeon in Germany who hunts a serial killer he once saved.

facts.quizQuestion: Who drew the artwork for Death Note while Tsugumi Ohba wrote the story?
facts.explanation: Takeshi Obata provided the artwork for Death Note, which ran in Weekly Shonen Jump from 2003 to 2006. Obata and Ohba also collaborated on Bakuman, a meta-manga about creating manga. Obata is known for his highly detailed character designs and expressive linework.

facts.quizQuestion: How many chapters did the Death Note manga run for in Weekly Shonen Jump between 2003 and 2006?
facts.explanation: Death Note ran for 108 chapters in Weekly Shonen Jump from December 2003 to May 2006, collected into 12 volumes. The series was adapted into a 37-episode anime by Madhouse in 2006 and has sold approximately 30 million copies worldwide.

facts.quizQuestion: How many chapters did Hajime Isayama's Attack on Titan manga run for before concluding in 2021?
facts.explanation: Attack on Titan ran for 139 chapters in Bessatsu Shonen Magazine (Kodansha) from September 2009 to April 2021. The manga sold over 110 million copies, making it one of the best-selling manga series ever, and its anime adaptation split production between Wit Studio and MAPPA.

facts.quizQuestion: In which Kodansha magazine was Attack on Titan serialized from 2009 to 2021?
facts.explanation: Attack on Titan was serialized in Bessatsu Shonen Magazine, a monthly manga anthology published by Kodansha. Hajime Isayama initially submitted the series concept to Weekly Shonen Jump but was rejected before it found its home at Kodansha.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio produced the original Neon Genesis Evangelion anime series that aired from 1995 to 1996?
facts.explanation: Gainax produced Neon Genesis Evangelion, directed by Hideaki Anno, which aired from October 1995 to March 1996 for 26 episodes. After the series, Anno founded Studio Khara to produce the Rebuild of Evangelion tetralogy, with the final film releasing in 2021.

facts.quizQuestion: Who directed Neon Genesis Evangelion, the landmark mecha anime that deconstructed the giant robot genre?
facts.explanation: Hideaki Anno directed Neon Genesis Evangelion, drawing on personal experiences with depression to craft a psychologically intense narrative within a mecha framework. Anno later founded Studio Khara and directed the Rebuild of Evangelion film series (2007–2021).

facts.quizQuestion: Which director created Cowboy Bebop, the 1998 jazz-influenced space western anime produced by Studio Sunrise?
facts.explanation: Shinichiro Watanabe directed Cowboy Bebop for Studio Sunrise, which aired in 1998 for 26 episodes. The series is renowned for its eclectic jazz and blues soundtrack composed by Yoko Kanno, and is widely considered one of the greatest and most accessible anime series for Western audiences.

facts.quizQuestion: Which composer created the eclectic jazz and blues soundtrack for Cowboy Bebop, featuring the iconic opening 'Tank!'?
facts.explanation: Yoko Kanno composed the Cowboy Bebop soundtrack, performing it with her band The Seatbelts. The score is celebrated for blending jazz, blues, rock, and classical music into a cohesive sonic identity. Kanno has also composed for Ghost in the Shell: Stand Alone Complex and Macross Frontier.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the landmark Akira anime film by Katsuhiro Otomo released, set in the cyberpunk city of Neo-Tokyo?
facts.explanation: Akira was released in 1988 with a then-unprecedented production budget of approximately 1.1 billion yen. Directed and adapted by manga creator Katsuhiro Otomo, the film set new standards for anime animation quality and became a defining work of the cyberpunk genre worldwide.

facts.quizQuestion: In which manga magazine was Katsuhiro Otomo's Akira serialized from 1982 to 1990?
facts.explanation: Akira was serialized in Young Magazine (Kodansha) from 1982 to 1990, running for approximately 2,000 pages across six volumes. The manga was notable for its detailed depiction of post-apocalyptic Tokyo and is considered a seminal work in the cyberpunk and science fiction genres.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the young Viking who seeks revenge against the mercenary who killed his father in Vinland Saga?
facts.explanation: Thorfinn is the protagonist of Vinland Saga by Makoto Yukimura, loosely inspired by the historical Norse explorer Thorfinn Karlsefni. The story begins with Thorfinn's obsession with revenge before evolving into a broader exploration of war, pacifism, and what it means to find a true homeland.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio took over production of Vinland Saga for Season 2 after Wit Studio produced the first season?
facts.explanation: MAPPA produced Vinland Saga Season 2 (2023), after Wit Studio had handled Season 1 (2019). MAPPA is the same studio behind Attack on Titan's final season and the Banana Fish anime, and has become one of the most prominent studios for prestige seinen adaptations.

acceptableAlternatives: Academy Award for Best Animated Feature
facts.quizQuestion: Spirited Away (2001) became the first anime to win an Academy Award — for which category?
facts.explanation: Spirited Away won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature at the 75th Oscars in 2003, the first and for many years the only anime to win in that category. It was directed by Hayao Miyazaki and produced by Studio Ghibli.

facts.quizQuestion: Which anime film finally surpassed Spirited Away as Japan's highest-grossing film in 2020?
facts.explanation: Spirited Away held the record as Japan's highest-grossing film from 2001 until 2020, when Demon Slayer: Mugen Train (ufotable) earned approximately ¥40.4 billion domestically. Spirited Away itself had previously displaced Titanic from that record.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Studio Ghibli co-founded by Hayao Miyazaki, Isao Takahata, and Toshio Suzuki?
facts.explanation: Studio Ghibli was founded in 1985 in Tokyo, Japan, following the success of Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind. The studio's name refers to the Italian word for a hot Saharan wind, reflecting Miyazaki's love of aviation.

distractors: Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind
facts.quizQuestion: What was the first official film produced by Studio Ghibli, released in 1986?
facts.explanation: Castle in the Sky (Laputa: Castle in the Sky) was Studio Ghibli's first official production, directed by Hayao Miyazaki and released in 1986. Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind (1984) predates the studio but was instrumental in its founding.

facts.quizQuestion: Totoro, the lovable forest spirit from the 1988 film, became the official mascot and logo of which studio?
facts.explanation: My Neighbor Totoro (1988), directed by Hayao Miyazaki, introduced the iconic Totoro character who became Studio Ghibli's mascot and appears in the studio's logo. The film was originally released as a double feature with Grave of the Fireflies.

distractors: Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind
facts.quizQuestion: Which 1997 Miyazaki film briefly held the record as Japan's highest-grossing film before being surpassed by Titanic?
facts.explanation: Princess Mononoke (1997) earned ¥19.3 billion at the Japanese box office, temporarily becoming Japan's highest-grossing film ever — until Titanic later that year. The film was a landmark for mature animated storytelling and environmental themes.

facts.quizQuestion: Who directed both My Neighbor Totoro (1988) and Spirited Away (2001)?
facts.explanation: Hayao Miyazaki is the co-founder of Studio Ghibli and director of its most beloved films including My Neighbor Totoro, Spirited Away, Princess Mononoke, and Howl's Moving Castle. He is widely regarded as one of the greatest animation directors of all time.

facts.quizQuestion: Who directed Grave of the Fireflies (1988), the anti-war Ghibli film often shown in Japanese schools?
facts.explanation: Grave of the Fireflies was directed by Isao Takahata, co-founder of Studio Ghibli. The film depicts two orphaned siblings struggling to survive in Japan during World War II and is widely regarded as one of the most powerful anti-war films ever made.

facts.correctAnswer: The Tale of the Princess Kaguya
facts.quizQuestion: Which 2013 Isao Takahata film is known for its distinctive hand-drawn watercolor animation style and received an Oscar nomination?
facts.explanation: The Tale of the Princess Kaguya (2013) was Isao Takahata's final film and received an Academy Award nomination for Best Animated Feature. It retells the ancient Japanese folk tale Taketori Monogatari using a distinctive sketchy, watercolor animation style.

distractors: The Girl Who Leapt Through Time
facts.quizQuestion: Which 2016 Makoto Shinkai film earned $380 million worldwide and became the highest-grossing anime film globally at the time of its release?
facts.explanation: Your Name (Kimi no Na wa, 2016), directed by Makoto Shinkai, grossed approximately $380 million worldwide, making it the highest-grossing anime film in the world at that time. The body-swap romance became a cultural phenomenon in Japan and internationally.

facts.quizQuestion: Your Name, Weathering with You, and Suzume were all produced by which animation studio?
facts.explanation: CoMix Wave Films has produced all of Makoto Shinkai's major theatrical films, including Your Name (2016), Weathering with You (2019), and Suzume (2022). The studio specializes in high-quality animation with detailed backgrounds and atmospheric lighting.

facts.quizQuestion: Which director of Your Name (2016) is often nicknamed 'the next Miyazaki'?
facts.explanation: Makoto Shinkai gained the nickname 'the next Miyazaki' after the massive success of Your Name (2016), which broke box office records for anime worldwide. His distinctive visual style features hyper-detailed backgrounds and emotionally-charged atmospheric lighting.

facts.quizQuestion: Which director's 1997 film Perfect Blue reportedly influenced Darren Aronofsky's Black Swan and Requiem for a Dream?
facts.explanation: Satoshi Kon directed Perfect Blue (1997), a psychological thriller about a pop idol whose grip on reality unravels. Darren Aronofsky reportedly purchased the rights to use specific shots from the film in Requiem for a Dream (2000) and cited it as an influence on Black Swan (2010).

distractors: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
facts.quizQuestion: Satoshi Kon's 2006 film Paprika is widely credited as an influence on which Christopher Nolan blockbuster about dream infiltration?
facts.explanation: Paprika (2006), directed by Satoshi Kon, depicts a device that allows therapists to enter patients' dreams. Christopher Nolan has acknowledged its influence on Inception (2010), and many film critics have noted striking visual and thematic parallels between the two films.

facts.quizQuestion: At what age did visionary director Satoshi Kon pass away from pancreatic cancer in 2010?
facts.explanation: Satoshi Kon died on August 24, 2010, at age 46 from pancreatic cancer, leaving behind four feature films — Perfect Blue, Millennium Actress, Tokyo Godfathers, and Paprika — and the TV series Paranoia Agent. His passing cut short a profoundly innovative career.

facts.quizQuestion: Which 1995 anime film directed by Mamoru Oshii is widely credited as a major influence on The Matrix?
facts.explanation: Ghost in the Shell (1995), directed by Mamoru Oshii, is a cyberpunk film exploring consciousness and identity in a future where humans merge with machines. The Wachowskis cited it as a primary visual and thematic influence on The Matrix (1999).

facts.quizQuestion: Who directed Akira (1988), the landmark cyberpunk anime film that was groundbreaking for its massive production budget?
facts.explanation: Akira (1988) was directed and written by Katsuhiro Otomo, based on his own manga series. With a production budget of ¥1.1 billion — unprecedented for anime at the time — it featured sophisticated animation and helped bring anime to global audiences.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Akira, the landmark cyberpunk anime directed by Katsuhiro Otomo, released?
facts.explanation: Akira was released in Japan on July 16, 1988. The same year also saw the release of My Neighbor Totoro and Grave of the Fireflies, making 1988 an extraordinary year for Japanese animation. Akira is set in a dystopian Neo-Tokyo in 2019.

distractors: The Tale of the Princess Kaguya
Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind
facts.quizQuestion: Which 2023 Miyazaki film won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature in 2024?
facts.explanation: The Boy and the Heron (2023), directed by Hayao Miyazaki and produced by Studio Ghibli, won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature at the 2024 ceremony. It represented Miyazaki's return to feature filmmaking after what was announced as his retirement following The Wind Rises (2013).

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio produced Demon Slayer: Mugen Train (2020), which became the highest-grossing Japanese film of all time?
facts.explanation: Demon Slayer: Mugen Train (2020) was produced by ufotable and earned approximately ¥40.4 billion in Japan, surpassing Spirited Away as the highest-grossing Japanese film ever at the time. ufotable is known for its fluid action sequences blending 2D and 3D animation.

facts.quizQuestion: Who founded Studio Chizu and directed The Girl Who Leapt Through Time (2006) and Wolf Children (2012)?
facts.explanation: Mamoru Hosoda directed The Girl Who Leapt Through Time (2006) and Summer Wars (2009) while at Madhouse, then founded his own studio, Studio Chizu, where he made Wolf Children (2012), The Boy and the Beast (2015), Mirai (2018), and Belle (2021).

facts.correctAnswer: Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind
facts.quizQuestion: Which 1984 Miyazaki film's commercial success directly led to the founding of Studio Ghibli?
facts.explanation: Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind (1984) was produced by Topcraft before Studio Ghibli existed, but its box office success gave Miyazaki and Takahata the leverage and funding to establish Studio Ghibli in 1985. The film is often considered an unofficial Ghibli film.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the ten-year-old protagonist who works in a spirit bathhouse in Spirited Away (2001)?
facts.explanation: Chihiro Ogino is the ten-year-old protagonist of Spirited Away who, after her parents are transformed into pigs, takes a job at a spirit bathhouse run by the witch Yubaba. She is given the work name 'Sen' by Yubaba as a way to suppress her identity.

facts.quizQuestion: In Princess Mononoke (1997), what is the name of the human girl raised by wolves who fights to protect the forest?
facts.explanation: San, nicknamed 'Princess Mononoke' (meaning spirit or monster princess), was raised by the wolf goddess Moro after her human parents abandoned her. She serves as a fierce protector of the forest against industrialization, and forms a complex bond with the film's hero Ashitaka.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Spirited Away released in Japan, where it became the country's highest-grossing film at the time?
facts.explanation: Spirited Away was released in Japan on July 20, 2001, and earned ¥31.7 billion domestically, making it the highest-grossing Japanese film at the time. It received wide international release in 2002–2003 and won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature in 2003.

facts.quizQuestion: What honorific title is Osamu Tezuka known by in the manga industry?
facts.explanation: Osamu Tezuka (1928–1989) earned the title 'God of Manga' for single-handedly transforming the medium into the cinematic storytelling form it is today. He introduced techniques like speed lines, close-ups, and dynamic panel flow that defined how manga looked for generations.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many pages of manga did Osamu Tezuka draw in his lifetime?
facts.explanation: Osamu Tezuka is estimated to have drawn over 150,000 pages of manga across more than 700 individual titles during his life. This extraordinary output required him to work through nights and weekends, aided by a team of assistants at his studio.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Osamu Tezuka's Astro Boy manga first begin serialization?
facts.explanation: Astro Boy (Tetsuwan Atom) began serialization in Shonen magazine in 1952 and ran until 1968. The story of a robot boy with human emotions became one of the most beloved manga series ever published and established many visual conventions still used in manga today.

facts.quizQuestion: Astro Boy became the first anime TV series to broadcast in Japan. What year did it premiere?
facts.explanation: Mushi Production's Astro Boy anime premiered on January 1, 1963, making it Japan's first domestically produced anime television series. It ran for 193 episodes and established the template for anime storytelling that all subsequent series would follow.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio did Osamu Tezuka found to produce Astro Boy?
facts.explanation: Osamu Tezuka founded Mushi Production in 1961 specifically to produce the Astro Boy anime series. The studio went on to produce several other landmark Tezuka adaptations including Kimba the White Lion and Black Jack before financial difficulties led to its closure in 1973.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the manga and anime series Mach GoGoGo, known in the West as Speed Racer?
facts.explanation: Tatsuo Yoshida created Mach GoGoGo (Speed Racer) as a manga in 1966, which Tatsunoko Production then adapted into a 52-episode anime series in 1967. The series was exported to the United States that same year, becoming one of the first anime series to reach American audiences.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produced the original Speed Racer (Mach GoGoGo) anime in 1967?
facts.explanation: Tatsunoko Production produced the 52-episode Mach GoGoGo anime in 1967. The studio was also behind other classic series including Science Ninja Team Gatchaman and Tekkaman. The Speed Racer export to the US helped open the American market to anime.

facts.quizQuestion: Who directed the original 1979 Mobile Suit Gundam anime series?
facts.explanation: Yoshiyuki Tomino directed Mobile Suit Gundam for Sunrise in 1979, inventing the 'real robot' genre of mecha anime. Rather than treating robots as superheroic tools, Tomino depicted them as military machinery in a realistic war setting, fundamentally changing science-fiction anime.

facts.quizQuestion: Who is the teenage protagonist who pilots the RX-78-2 Gundam in the original 1979 Mobile Suit Gundam series?
facts.explanation: Amuro Ray is the reluctant teenage hero of Mobile Suit Gundam who pilots the RX-78-2 Gundam against the Zeon forces. His character arc from frightened teenager to skilled Newtype soldier was groundbreaking for anime and established the template for mecha protagonists.

facts.quizQuestion: Under what pen name did the creator of Lupin III publish his manga?
facts.explanation: Kazuhiko Kato created the Lupin III manga under the pen name Monkey Punch, beginning serialization in Weekly Manga Action in 1967. The pen name was intended to sound Western, fitting the series' French thief protagonist. The manga spawned one of the longest-running anime franchises in Japan.

acceptableAlternatives: Lupin III: The Castle of Cagliostro
distractors: Nausicaa of the Valley of the Wind
facts.quizQuestion: Which 1979 Lupin III film was Hayao Miyazaki's feature film directorial debut?
facts.explanation: The Castle of Cagliostro (1979) was Hayao Miyazaki's theatrical directorial debut, though he had previously co-directed a TV special. The film followed gentleman thief Lupin III on an adventure involving a counterfeiting operation and a medieval castle. It is now considered a classic of action animation.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the manga series Doraemon?
facts.explanation: Doraemon was created by the duo Fujiko F. Fujio — pen name for Hiroshi Fujimoto and Motoo Abiko — with Fujimoto being the primary creative force behind the series. The manga began in 1969 and introduced the blue robotic cat from the future who helps a boy named Nobita with gadgets from his 4D pocket.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Doraemon manga first begin publication?
facts.explanation: Doraemon began serialization in December 1969 and ran until 1996, a span of over 26 years. The series was eventually collected into 45 volumes and published in CoroCoro Comic. Its anime adaptation began in 1979 and has been running for over 2,800 episodes, making it one of the longest anime series ever.

facts.quizQuestion: In which magazine did Captain Tsubasa first run from 1981 to 1988?
facts.explanation: Captain Tsubasa by Yoichi Takahashi ran in Weekly Shonen Jump from 1981 to 1988 and is credited with inspiring a generation of professional footballers worldwide. Players including Zinedine Zidane, Alessandro Del Piero, and Fernando Torres have cited the series as a childhood influence on their love of soccer.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Fist of the North Star character delivers the iconic line 'You are already dead'?
facts.explanation: Kenshiro, the protagonist of Fist of the North Star, delivers the now-legendary catchphrase 'Omae wa mou shindeiru' (You are already dead) after striking opponents with his Hokuto Shinken technique. The enemy then realizes they have been killed and explodes. The phrase became one of anime's most recognized and parodied moments.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Fist of the North Star begin serialization in Weekly Shonen Jump?
facts.explanation: Fist of the North Star (Hokuto no Ken) began in Weekly Shonen Jump in 1983, written by Buronson and illustrated by Tetsuo Hara. Set in a post-nuclear wasteland, the series ran until 1988 and was adapted into a 152-episode anime by Toei Animation that same year it launched.

facts.quizQuestion: Who is the illustrator of the Fist of the North Star manga?
facts.explanation: Tetsuo Hara drew the muscular, explosive art for Fist of the North Star while Buronson (Sho Fumimura) wrote the script. Hara's dynamic battle scenes and hyper-detailed character designs became enormously influential on action manga throughout the 1980s and beyond.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the Saint Seiya manga, which ran in Weekly Shonen Jump from 1986 to 1990?
facts.explanation: Masami Kurumada created Saint Seiya, a series following warriors in mystical armors called Cloths inspired by Greek constellations. The Toei Animation adaptation became enormously popular in Latin America, France, and Southern Europe, building a fanbase that rivals Dragon Ball in those regions.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Space Battleship Yamato first air in Japan?
facts.explanation: Space Battleship Yamato premiered in Japan in 1974 and became one of the earliest serious space opera anime. Known as Star Blazers in the West, the series depicted the WWII battleship Yamato reimagined as a starship. It directly influenced the development of Mobile Suit Gundam and later science fiction anime.

facts.quizQuestion: By what title was the anime series Space Battleship Yamato broadcast in the United States?
facts.explanation: Space Battleship Yamato was localized for American audiences under the title Star Blazers beginning in 1979. It was notable for being one of the first anime to keep its ongoing serialized narrative largely intact for US broadcast, rather than being heavily re-edited as was common at the time.

facts.quizQuestion: Mobile Suit Gundam (1979) is credited with creating which mecha sub-genre, depicting robots as military weapons rather than superheroic tools?
facts.explanation: Mobile Suit Gundam established the 'real robot' genre by treating its giant robots as military hardware with fuel limits, ammunition, and mechanical failures — in contrast to the 'super robot' genre popularized by Mazinger Z, where robots were near-invincible weapons of heroic destiny. This grounded approach transformed anime storytelling.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Osamu Tezuka's Kimba the White Lion anime first air?
facts.explanation: Kimba the White Lion (Jungle Taitei) premiered in 1965, adapted from Tezuka's manga of the same name. Produced by Mushi Production, it was also one of the first color anime television series broadcast in Japan. The series later became internationally controversial when The Lion King was released by Disney in 1994.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the peak weekly circulation of Weekly Shonen Jump, reached in 1995?
facts.explanation: Weekly Shonen Jump hit 6.53 million copies per week in 1995, its all-time peak. This record was driven by simultaneous top-tier serializations including Dragon Ball, Slam Dunk, and Yu Yu Hakusho.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Shueisha first publish Weekly Shonen Jump?
facts.explanation: Weekly Shonen Jump launched in 1968 and quickly became the most influential manga magazine in history. It went on to serialize iconic series such as Dragon Ball, One Piece, Naruto, and Demon Slayer.

facts.quizQuestion: Which publisher owns Weekly Shonen Jump and serializes One Piece and Naruto?
facts.explanation: Shueisha is the publisher of Weekly Shonen Jump and home to many of the best-selling manga of all time, including One Piece and Naruto. It is part of the Hitotsubashi Group alongside rival publisher Shogakukan.

facts.quizQuestion: Which of Japan's Big Three manga publishers was founded in 1909, making it the oldest of the group?
facts.explanation: Kodansha was founded in 1909 and is the oldest of Japan's three major manga publishers. It publishes Weekly Shonen Magazine and is home to series like Attack on Titan and Fairy Tail.

facts.quizQuestion: Which publisher serializes Detective Conan and Doraemon, and also publishes Weekly Shonen Sunday?
facts.explanation: Shogakukan is the publisher behind Weekly Shonen Sunday and iconic series including Detective Conan (Case Closed) and Doraemon. Along with Shueisha, it is part of the Hitotsubashi Group.

facts.quizQuestion: What manga demographic term refers to series primarily aimed at boys aged 12–18?
facts.explanation: Shonen (少年) literally means 'boy' in Japanese and is the demographic label for manga targeting boys roughly aged 12 to 18. Weekly Shonen Jump, Shonen Sunday, and Shonen Magazine are the three largest shonen publications.

facts.quizQuestion: What manga demographic term describes series targeting young adult men between roughly 18 and 40 years old?
facts.explanation: Seinen (青年) means 'young man' in Japanese and labels manga aimed at men roughly aged 18 to 40. Publications such as Young Jump, Big Comic Spirits, Young Animal, and Morning carry seinen content.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the demographic label for manga aimed at girls aged 12–18, published in magazines like Nakayoshi and Ribon?
facts.explanation: Shojo (少女) means 'girl' in Japanese and designates manga targeted at girls roughly aged 12 to 18. Key shojo magazines include Nakayoshi, Ribon, and LaLa, which have published beloved series such as Sailor Moon and Cardcaptor Sakura.

facts.quizQuestion: What manga demographic label covers series primarily targeting adult women aged 18 and older?
facts.explanation: Josei (女性) means 'woman' in Japanese and designates manga targeting adult women aged 18 and up. Josei magazines such as Feel Young, Cookie, and Kiss tend to feature more realistic portrayals of romance and adult life compared to shojo titles.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the Japanese term for the collected volume format in which serialized manga chapters are compiled and sold as a standalone book?
facts.explanation: A tankōbon (単行本) is a standalone collected volume compiling chapters previously serialized in a manga magazine. Volumes typically run 180–200 pages and are released every two to four months, giving fans a durable format after the disposable magazine run.

facts.quizQuestion: What adhesive sheet material covered in dot or texture patterns do manga artists apply to artwork to create shading and tonal effects?
facts.explanation: Screentone (スクリーントーン) sheets carry repeating patterns of dots, lines, or textures that simulate tonal shading without gray paint in black-and-white manga. Artists cut pieces of these adhesive sheets and apply them to inked artwork; digital screentone tools are now widely used as well.

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the radial lines drawn from a focal point in manga panels to convey motion, impact, or intense focus?
facts.explanation: Speed lines (集中線, shūchūsen) are radial lines emanating from a central point that manga artists use to suggest rapid movement, dramatic impact, or a character's heightened concentration. They became a defining visual convention of action and shonen manga.

facts.quizQuestion: What art style, also called 'super-deformed,' depicts characters in exaggerated cute proportions with oversized heads and tiny bodies for comedic effect?
facts.explanation: Chibi (ちび), also known as super-deformed or SD, is an art style where characters are drawn with extremely enlarged heads and tiny bodies. Manga artists typically switch to chibi momentarily for comedic or cute interludes before returning to the regular art style.

facts.quizQuestion: What mechanism does Weekly Shonen Jump use to determine which serialized manga survive or get cancelled each week?
facts.explanation: Weekly Shonen Jump uses reader surveys (アンケート, ankēto) collected from each weekly issue to rank ongoing series. Series that consistently rank low face cancellation, which creates a high-pressure environment that has also produced some of the most popular manga ever written.

facts.quizQuestion: What biannual self-publishing event held in Tokyo is the world's largest fan convention and primary marketplace for doujinshi?
facts.explanation: Comiket (Comic Market) is a biannual self-publishing fair held at Tokyo Big Sight that attracts over 700,000 attendees per event and serves as the main venue for buying and selling doujinshi. Many professional mangaka, including members of CLAMP, began their careers by selling doujinshi at Comiket.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many yen does a single issue of Weekly Shonen Jump cost at Japanese newsstands?
facts.explanation: A single issue of Weekly Shonen Jump costs approximately 280 yen, equivalent to roughly $2 USD, making it an extremely affordable weekly read. The low cover price is possible because the bulk of the magazine's revenue comes from advertising and tankōbon volume sales.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the Tezuka Osamu Cultural Prize established to honor outstanding manga named after the 'God of Manga'?
facts.explanation: The Tezuka Osamu Cultural Prize was established in 1997 and is named after Tezuka Osamu, widely regarded as the 'God of Manga' for revolutionizing the medium. Notable winners include One Piece and Slam Dunk.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the Shogakukan Manga Award first presented, making it the oldest manga award in Japan?
facts.explanation: The Shogakukan Manga Award has been presented since 1955, making it the oldest ongoing manga award in Japan. It recognizes excellence across both children's and general manga categories and has honored landmark series across many decades.

facts.quizQuestion: One Piece, the best-selling manga series of all time, is published by which company?
facts.explanation: One Piece by Eiichiro Oda is published by Shueisha and serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump since 1997. It is the best-selling manga series in history, with over 520 million volumes in circulation worldwide as of 2023.

facts.quizQuestion: Attack on Titan by Hajime Isayama was serialized in Bessatsu Shonen Magazine, published by which company?
facts.explanation: Attack on Titan (進撃の巨人) was serialized in Bessatsu Shonen Magazine, a publication of Kodansha, from 2009 to 2021. The series became one of the best-selling manga of all time with over 140 million copies in circulation.

facts.quizQuestion: In what direction are manga pages and panels traditionally read, mirrored from the Western book convention?
facts.explanation: Manga is traditionally read right to left — the spine is on the right, and panels flow from the top-right corner to the bottom-left. When manga is localized for Western markets, publishers must choose between mirroring the art (flipping) or keeping the original orientation with a right-to-left reader instruction.

facts.quizQuestion: Which all-female manga artist collective began their career selling doujinshi at Comiket before going on to create Cardcaptor Sakura and xxxHolic?
facts.explanation: CLAMP is an all-female manga artist collective that started by selling doujinshi (fan-made comics) at Comiket events in the 1980s. They later became professional mangaka and created acclaimed series including Cardcaptor Sakura, X/1999, and xxxHolic.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Studio Ghibli founded by Hayao Miyazaki, Isao Takahata, and Toshio Suzuki?
facts.explanation: Studio Ghibli was established in June 1985 in Tokyo, shortly after the theatrical success of Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind. The name 'Ghibli' comes from an Italian word for a hot Saharan wind, reflecting Miyazaki's love of aviation.

facts.quizQuestion: Toei Animation, the oldest major anime studio (founded 1948), earned what nickname due to its massive output and industry dominance?
facts.explanation: Toei Animation is often called 'the Disney of the East' because of its enormous scale, its role in establishing the anime industry, and its production of iconic long-running series. Toei has produced Dragon Ball, Sailor Moon, One Piece, and the Precure franchise.

facts.quizQuestion: Which producer co-founded Madhouse in 1972 and later went on to found MAPPA in 2011?
facts.explanation: Masao Maruyama co-founded Madhouse in 1972 after leaving Mushi Production, where he had worked under Osamu Tezuka. Decades later, in 2011, he founded MAPPA (Maruyama Animation Produce Project Association), which has since produced Attack on Titan's final season, Jujutsu Kaisen, and Chainsaw Man.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Trigger founded by Hiroyuki Imaishi and other former Gainax staff?
facts.explanation: Studio Trigger was founded in 2011 by Hiroyuki Imaishi and Masahiko Ohtsuka after they departed from Gainax. The studio debuted with Kill la Kill in 2013 and has since produced Promare and the Netflix series Cyberpunk: Edgerunners.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Osamu Tezuka-founded studio pioneered the limited animation technique in the 1960s, shaping the visual language of anime?
facts.explanation: Mushi Production, founded by Osamu Tezuka in 1962, popularized limited animation for television — using fewer frames per second (often 8-12 vs Disney's 24), held cels, speed lines, and dramatic stills to cut costs while maintaining expressive storytelling. This became the defining visual style of anime.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did a devastating arson attack kill 36 people at Kyoto Animation's studio building?
facts.explanation: On July 18, 2019, an arsonist set fire to Kyoto Animation's Studio 1 building, killing 36 employees and injuring 33 others. It was one of Japan's deadliest mass killings in decades. The global anime community rallied with an outpouring of donations and support for the studio.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio produced Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba and is known for its industry-leading digital animation and visual effects?
facts.explanation: ufotable, founded in 2000, is renowned for its seamless integration of 2D and 3D CGI, producing stunning water, flame, and breath effects in Demon Slayer that became viral moments worldwide. The studio is also known for the Fate franchise animated adaptations.

facts.quizQuestion: In anime fan culture, what does the Japanese term 'sakuga' (作画) refer to?
facts.explanation: Literally meaning 'drawing' or 'animation,' sakuga in fan circles refers to exceptionally fluid and expressive animation sequences, often by star key animators. Websites like sakugabooru.com catalog and credit these outstanding cuts, elevating individual animators to celebrity status within the community.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio, founded in 1998 by ex-Sunrise staff, has produced My Hero Academia, Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood, and Mob Psycho 100?
facts.explanation: Bones was founded in 1998 by Sunrise veterans including Masahiko Minami. The studio became known for high-quality original works and manga adaptations, earning particular acclaim for Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood's faithful retelling and My Hero Academia's long-running heroic action.

facts.quizQuestion: What financing structure, where publishers, studios, distributors, and toy companies pool resources and share profits, dominates anime production in Japan?
facts.explanation: The production committee system (製作委員会, seisaku iinkai) spreads financial risk across multiple stakeholders — each contributing funding in exchange for rights to exploit specific revenue streams such as home video, merchandise, music, or overseas licensing. This structure has shaped what kinds of anime get made, often favoring established IP over risky originals.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio, founded in 1972 and now known as Bandai Namco Filmworks, created the Mobile Suit Gundam franchise and produced Cowboy Bebop?
facts.explanation: Sunrise was founded in 1972 as a subsidiary of Bandai and became famous for creating the Mobile Suit Gundam franchise in 1979 with director Yoshiyuki Tomino. The studio also produced iconic works including Cowboy Bebop and Code Geass before being renamed Bandai Namco Filmworks in 2022.

facts.quizQuestion: Anime's limited animation technique typically uses what frame rate, compared to Disney's standard of 24 frames per second?
facts.explanation: Limited animation runs at roughly 8-12 frames per second (sometimes called 'on twos' or 'on threes'), compared to the 24 fps of full Disney-style animation. The reduced frame count is compensated with expressive poses, speed lines, reaction shots, and dynamic camera work that became anime's signature visual grammar.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was MAPPA founded by Masao Maruyama after his departure from Madhouse?
facts.explanation: MAPPA (Maruyama Animation Produce Project Association) was founded in 2011 by Masao Maruyama, who had previously co-founded Madhouse in 1972. Since its founding, MAPPA has produced some of the most talked-about anime of the 2020s, including the final season of Attack on Titan, Jujutsu Kaisen, and Chainsaw Man.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio, known for the Monogatari series and Puella Magi Madoka Magica, is famous for its distinctive 'head tilt' directorial style?
facts.explanation: Studio Shaft, founded in 1975, developed a signature visual style under director Akiyuki Shinbo characterized by dramatic head tilts, unconventional camera angles, abstract background art, and heavy use of text on screen. This style became so associated with the studio that fans coined the term 'Shaft head tilt' for the recurring pose.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio, founded in 2012, animated the first three seasons of Attack on Titan before MAPPA took over for the final season?
facts.explanation: Wit Studio was founded in 2012 as a subsidiary of Production I.G and produced Attack on Titan from season 1 (2013) through season 3 (2019), as well as Vinland Saga season 1 and the co-production of Spy x Family with CloverWorks. The studio is known for its high production values and dynamic action sequences.

facts.quizQuestion: In anime production, what role is responsible for drawing the important poses that define a movement, with other artists filling in the frames between?
facts.explanation: Key animators (genga-man) draw the essential 'extremes' or anchor poses of a movement. In-between animators (douga-man) then draw all the intermediate frames to create smooth motion. Star key animators are celebrated in fan communities for their distinctive styles, and their work is cataloged on sites like sakugabooru.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Neon Genesis Evangelion opening theme song became one of the most sung karaoke songs in Japan?
facts.explanation: "Cruel Angel's Thesis" (残酷な天使のテーゼ), performed by Yoko Takahashi and first aired in 1995, became a karaoke staple across Japan for decades. The song regularly appears in lists of Japan's most-sung karaoke songs, partly due to its broad emotional resonance and singable melody.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio, known for K-On! and Violet Evergarden, is also praised for offering unusually good working conditions and in-house animator training?
facts.explanation: Kyoto Animation (KyoAni), founded in 1981, operates differently from most anime studios by training animators in-house and employing most of its staff directly rather than relying on freelancers — a rare practice in an industry known for poor pay and overwork. This model contributes to its consistently high animation quality.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately when did the anime industry complete its transition from hand-painted cels to digital coloring and compositing?
facts.explanation: The anime industry broadly shifted from physical cel animation to digital production around the year 2000, driven by falling computer costs and software advances. Most studios adopted digital coloring and compositing through the late 1990s; the long-running Sazae-san was among the last holdouts, finally switching to digital in 2013.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio, founded in 1987, animated the original Ghost in the Shell films and later produced Haikyuu!! and Psycho-Pass?
facts.explanation: Production I.G was founded in 1987 by Mitsuhisa Ishikawa and gained international recognition through Mamoru Oshii's Ghost in the Shell (1995), which influenced filmmakers worldwide including the Wachowskis (The Matrix). The studio later produced the beloved sports anime Haikyuu!! and the dystopian thriller Psycho-Pass.

facts.quizQuestion: Which anime studio produced the Netflix series Cyberpunk: Edgerunners, based on CD Projekt Red's Cyberpunk 2077?
facts.explanation: Studio Trigger, led by director Hiroyuki Imaishi, produced Cyberpunk: Edgerunners in 2022 for Netflix. The series became a cultural phenomenon, reviving interest in the Cyberpunk 2077 game and earning widespread critical acclaim for its kinetic action, vivid color palette, and emotional story.

facts.quizQuestion: Which director left Gainax to co-found Studio Trigger in 2011 and later directed Kill la Kill and Promare?
facts.explanation: Hiroyuki Imaishi built his reputation at Gainax directing Gurren Lagann and the Panty and Stocking with Garterbelt series. He co-founded Trigger in 2011 with Masahiko Ohtsuka, then directed Kill la Kill (2013) and the theatrical film Promare (2019), both celebrated for their maximalist, high-energy animation style.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Comiket (Comic Market), the world's largest fan convention, first held in Tokyo?
facts.explanation: Comiket was founded in 1975 in Tokyo as a marketplace for doujinshi (self-published fan works). It now draws over 750,000 attendees across three days each summer and winter event at Tokyo Big Sight.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Nobuyuki Takahashi coin the term "cosplay" after attending World Science Fiction Convention in Los Angeles?
facts.explanation: Nobuyuki Takahashi coined the portmanteau of "costume" and "play" in 1984 after attending Worldcon in Los Angeles. The term spread globally and cosplay is now a worldwide phenomenon with professional practitioners and international competitions.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Spirited Away become the first anime film to win the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature?
facts.explanation: Hayao Miyazaki's Spirited Away won the Oscar for Best Animated Feature at the 2003 ceremony, making it the first anime film ever to receive that honor. The film remains one of the highest-grossing anime films of all time in Japan.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Crunchyroll founded, the streaming platform that would become the world's largest dedicated anime service?
facts.explanation: Crunchyroll was founded in 2006, originally as a platform hosting unlicensed anime uploads before transitioning to a legitimate licensed service. It was acquired by Sony/Aniplex and as of 2024 has over 13 million paid subscribers worldwide.

facts.quizQuestion: Which anime film did the Wachowskis cite as their primary visual and conceptual influence when creating The Matrix (1999)?
facts.explanation: The Wachowski siblings explicitly cited the 1995 anime film Ghost in the Shell as a major influence on The Matrix, particularly its visual style, philosophical themes about the nature of consciousness, and action sequences. The similarity is most evident in the film's bullet-time effects and cyberspace imagery.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Netflix live-action anime adaptation was considered a rare success and renewed for a second season in 2023?
facts.explanation: Netflix's live-action adaptation of One Piece (2023) was praised for its faithfulness to the source material and received strong viewership numbers, leading to a renewal — a rarity among live-action anime adaptations, which typically receive poor reviews and cancellations.

facts.quizQuestion: Which 2022 Netflix anime produced by Studio Trigger dramatically boosted sales for the Cyberpunk 2077 video game upon its release?
facts.explanation: Cyberpunk: Edgerunners (2022), produced by Studio Trigger in collaboration with CD Projekt Red, was so well-received that it caused a major resurgence in Cyberpunk 2077 player counts and sales. The anime is set in the same Night City universe and follows a new protagonist.

distractors: Nausicaa of the Valley of the Wind
facts.quizQuestion: Which Hayao Miyazaki film won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature in 2003, becoming the first anime to receive that honor?
facts.explanation: Spirited Away, known in Japan as Sen to Chihiro no Kamikakushi, is the only non-English animated film to ever win the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature. The film follows a young girl navigating a spirit world to save her parents.

facts.quizQuestion: Who coined the term "cosplay" in 1984, creating the word that now defines an entire global subculture?
facts.explanation: Nobuyuki Takahashi, a journalist for My Anime magazine, coined the word "cosplay" (コスプレ) in 1984 after reporting on costumed fans at the Los Angeles World Science Fiction Convention. His portmanteau of "costume" and "play" spread through Japan's otaku community and eventually worldwide.

facts.quizQuestion: Which anime director has won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature twice, with Spirited Away (2003) and The Boy and the Heron (2024)?
facts.explanation: Hayao Miyazaki, co-founder of Studio Ghibli, became the first person to win the Best Animated Feature Oscar twice. His 2024 win for The Boy and the Heron came after he had announced retirement multiple times. He is widely considered one of the greatest animation directors in history.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Tokyo district is considered the global cultural center of otaku culture, known for its anime merchandise shops and maid cafes?
facts.explanation: Akihabara (秋葉原), often called "Akiba," is Tokyo's hub for electronics, anime, manga, and video game merchandise. The district evolved from a postwar electronics market into the spiritual home of otaku culture, featuring multi-floor anime shops, gashapon machines, and themed maid cafes.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the English translation of 聖地巡礼 (seichi junrei), the practice of fans visiting real-world locations depicted in anime?
facts.explanation: Seichi junrei (聖地巡礼), literally "sacred place pilgrimage," describes the phenomenon of anime fans traveling to real locations featured in their favorite series. Towns like Chichibu (Your Name), Toyosato (K-On!), and Oarai (Girls und Panzer) saw dramatic tourism boosts as a result.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many attendees does Comiket (Comic Market) draw per event over its three-day run?
facts.explanation: Comiket consistently draws over 750,000 attendees per event, making it the largest fan convention in the world by attendance. The event is held twice yearly at Tokyo Big Sight and features over 35,000 circles (seller groups) primarily offering doujinshi (self-published works).

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many paid subscribers does Crunchyroll have as of 2024, making it the world's largest dedicated anime streaming platform?
facts.explanation: Crunchyroll reported over 13 million paid subscribers as of 2024 after merging with Funimation under Sony/Aniplex ownership. The platform offers simulcast streaming of new anime episodes within hours of their Japanese broadcast, serving fans in over 200 countries.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately what percentage of all books and magazines published in Japan is accounted for by manga?
facts.explanation: Manga accounts for roughly 25% of all print publications in Japan, demonstrating its deep integration into Japanese media culture. This massive domestic market has historically made Japan the world's leading producer and exporter of comic content.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the Japanese term for self-published fan works, primarily sold at events like Comiket, often featuring characters from existing anime and manga?
facts.explanation: Doujinshi (同人誌) are self-published works, typically manga, produced by amateur or semi-professional creators. While many feature fan-made stories using characters from commercial properties, original doujinshi also exist. Comiket exists primarily as a marketplace for doujinshi creators.

facts.quizQuestion: What Japanese term, originally meaning "someone obsessed with a particular topic," was embraced by Western anime fans as a self-identifier?
facts.explanation: "Otaku" (おたく) originally carried a somewhat pejorative connotation in Japan, implying extreme obsession and social awkwardness. In Western fan communities, however, the term was broadly adopted as a positive self-identifier for anime and manga enthusiasts, reversing its negative nuance.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of Japan's government initiative launched in the 2010s that promotes anime, manga, and pop culture as tools of cultural diplomacy?
facts.explanation: The "Cool Japan" (クールジャパン) initiative is a Japanese government soft power strategy that promotes Japanese popular culture — including anime, manga, fashion, and food — to boost cultural influence and tourism abroad. The Japan Foundation plays a key role in distributing this content internationally.

facts.quizQuestion: What is a 漫画喫茶 (manga kissa), a type of establishment popular in Japan where patrons pay by the hour to read from an extensive collection?
facts.explanation: Manga kissaten (漫画喫茶), often shortened to "manga kissa," are pay-by-the-hour establishments where customers can read from thousands of manga volumes. They often provide private cubicles, internet access, and light refreshments, serving both manga fans and people needing a quiet place to rest.

facts.quizQuestion: In which Japanese city has the World Cosplay Summit been held annually since 2003?
facts.explanation: The World Cosplay Summit has been held in Nagoya, Japan every year since its founding in 2003. The competition brings together top cosplayers from dozens of countries to compete, and it is supported by the Japanese government as part of cultural diplomacy efforts.

facts.quizQuestion: What is a gashapon (ガシャポン), the coin-operated machines ubiquitous in Japanese arcades and anime shops that dispense small collectibles?
facts.explanation: Gashapon (ガシャポン), named for the sound the machine makes, are coin-operated vending machines dispensing small plastic capsules containing random miniature figures or toys. They are a staple of Akihabara shops and arcades, with anime and manga characters being among the most popular gashapon themes.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Japanese animation studio animated the anime sequence in Quentin Tarantino's Kill Bill: Volume 1 (2003)?
facts.explanation: Quentin Tarantino hired Production I.G, the studio behind Ghost in the Shell, to animate the "Oren Ishii Origin" sequence in Kill Bill: Volume 1. The collaboration demonstrated anime's growing mainstream recognition in Hollywood and cemented Production I.G's reputation for cinematic quality animation.

facts.quizQuestion: Who is the creator of the manga series Spy x Family?
facts.explanation: Tatsuya Endo began serializing Spy x Family in Shueisha's digital magazine Shonen Jump+ in March 2019. The story follows a spy, an assassin, and a telepath child all pretending to be a normal family.

facts.quizQuestion: Which two animation studios co-produced the Spy x Family anime that premiered in 2022?
facts.explanation: The Spy x Family anime adaptation that premiered in April 2022 was a rare co-production between Wit Studio and CloverWorks. The two studios split production duties across the cours of the series.

facts.quizQuestion: By 2023, approximately how many million copies of the Spy x Family manga were in circulation?
facts.explanation: Spy x Family surpassed 34 million copies in circulation by 2023, making it one of the fastest-growing manga series of its era. The series' breakout character Anya Forger became a widely beloved internet meme.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the manga Chainsaw Man, serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump starting in 2018?
facts.explanation: Tatsuki Fujimoto created Chainsaw Man, which ran in Weekly Shonen Jump from 2018 to 2020 for Part 1, then moved to Shueisha's digital platform Jump+ for Part 2 starting in 2022. MAPPA produced the anime adaptation that premiered in October 2022.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produced the Chainsaw Man anime that premiered in 2022?
facts.explanation: MAPPA produced the Chainsaw Man anime adaptation, which premiered in October 2022 to widespread acclaim. The studio also produces Attack on Titan's final season and Jujutsu Kaisen, making it one of the most prominent anime studios of the early 2020s.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the protagonist in Chainsaw Man who merges with a chainsaw devil?
facts.explanation: Denji is the protagonist of Chainsaw Man, a young man who merges with his pet devil Pochita and gains the power to manifest chainsaws from his body. The series is known for its dark, subversive take on shonen action tropes.

facts.quizQuestion: Solo Leveling, one of the world's most popular manhwa series, originally comes from which country?
facts.explanation: Solo Leveling originated as a South Korean web novel by Chugong before being adapted into a manhwa (Korean comic). Its global popularity helped introduce millions of international readers to manhwa as a format distinct from Japanese manga.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the protagonist in Solo Leveling who starts as the weakest hunter and gains the ability to level up?
facts.explanation: Sung Jinwoo is the protagonist of Solo Leveling, initially ranked as the weakest hunter in the world until he is chosen as a 'Player' by a mysterious system that grants him the unique ability to grow stronger through leveling up. The A-1 Pictures anime adaptation premiered in January 2024.

facts.quizQuestion: Which animation studio produced the Solo Leveling anime that premiered in January 2024?
facts.explanation: A-1 Pictures produced the Solo Leveling anime adaptation, which premiered in January 2024 and became one of the most anticipated anime of the year. A-1 Pictures is also known for producing Sword Art Online, Fairy Tail, and various other major titles.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the Sword Art Online light novel series that began in 2009?
facts.explanation: Reki Kawahara originally published Sword Art Online as a web novel in 2002 before it was picked up for official publication as a light novel in 2009. The A-1 Pictures anime adaptation that aired in 2012 became a massive hit and helped popularize the isekai and game-world subgenres.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the nickname of the protagonist Kazuto Kirigaya in Sword Art Online?
facts.explanation: Kazuto Kirigaya uses the in-game username Kirito in Sword Art Online. As a solo 'beater' player who becomes one of the most powerful swordsmen in the virtual world, Kirito became one of the most recognizable anime protagonists of the 2010s.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Dragon Ball Z character delivers the iconic 'It's Over 9000!' line while crushing a scouter?
facts.explanation: Vegeta delivers the famous line while scanning Goku's power level in the English dub of Dragon Ball Z, though the original Japanese line was actually 'It's over 8000!' The discrepancy became a notable piece of anime localization trivia and an enduring internet meme.

facts.quizQuestion: After which historical figure is Goku's signature Kamehameha energy attack named?
facts.explanation: The Kamehameha attack is named after King Kamehameha I, the Hawaiian monarch who unified the Hawaiian Islands in the early 19th century. Akira Toriyama named Master Roshi's technique after the king because he liked the sound of the word; the move has since become one of the most iconic energy attacks in all of anime.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the pen name of the artist who originally created One Punch Man as a webcomic in 2009?
facts.explanation: ONE created One Punch Man as a self-published webcomic starting in 2009. The story's premise — a hero so powerful he defeats every enemy in one punch and finds no satisfaction in fighting — became wildly popular, leading manga artist Yusuke Murata to create a high-quality manga remake starting in 2012.

facts.quizQuestion: Which manga artist produced the highly acclaimed redraw of One Punch Man, renowned for its spectacular action artwork?
facts.explanation: Yusuke Murata, previously known for Eyeshield 21, produced the official manga adaptation of One Punch Man starting in 2012 with art so detailed and dynamic that it set a new standard for action manga illustration. Madhouse then produced the Season 1 anime in 2015 to critical acclaim.

facts.quizQuestion: Which studio produced the critically acclaimed Season 1 of One Punch Man in 2015?
facts.explanation: Madhouse produced the first season of the One Punch Man anime in 2015, which became famous for its fluid, high-quality animation particularly during action sequences. The series is a satire of shonen power escalation, with protagonist Saitama able to defeat any enemy with a single punch.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the Tokyo Ghoul manga series, which ran in Weekly Young Jump from 2011 to 2018?
facts.explanation: Sui Ishida created Tokyo Ghoul, which serialized in Shueisha's Weekly Young Jump magazine from 2011 to 2014, followed by the sequel Tokyo Ghoul:re from 2014 to 2018. The Pierrot anime adaptation introduced the dark urban fantasy story to a global audience.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the half-ghoul protagonist of Tokyo Ghoul who becomes caught between human and ghoul worlds?
facts.explanation: Ken Kaneki is a college student who becomes a half-ghoul after a near-fatal encounter, forcing him to navigate life between human society and the hidden ghoul world. His transformation, including his iconic white hair, became one of anime's most recognizable character design moments.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the basketball manga Slam Dunk, which ran in Weekly Shonen Jump from 1990 to 1996?
facts.explanation: Takehiko Inoue created Slam Dunk, which ran for 276 chapters in Weekly Shonen Jump and is widely credited with popularizing basketball throughout Japan. The manga has sold over 170 million copies, and Inoue revisited the franchise with the 2022 film The First Slam Dunk.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many million copies of the Slam Dunk manga have been sold worldwide?
facts.explanation: Slam Dunk by Takehiko Inoue has sold over 170 million copies worldwide, making it one of the best-selling manga series of all time. The manga single-handedly sparked a basketball boom in Japan during the 1990s.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the title of the 2022 Slam Dunk theatrical film that earned over $250 million worldwide?
facts.explanation: The First Slam Dunk, released in December 2022 and directed by original manga creator Takehiko Inoue, earned over $256 million worldwide. It became one of the highest-grossing anime films ever and introduced the franchise to a new generation of fans.

facts.quizQuestion: What does the Japanese word 'isekai' (異世界) literally mean in English?
facts.explanation: Isekai literally means 'another world' or 'different world' in Japanese, referring to the genre where a protagonist is transported to a fantasy world. The genre exploded in popularity in the 2010s, with series like Sword Art Online, Re:Zero, KonoSuba, and That Time I Got Reincarnated as a Slime.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the detective manga Detective Conan (Case Closed), which has been serialized since 1994?
facts.explanation: Gosho Aoyama began serializing Detective Conan in Shogakukan's Weekly Shonen Sunday in January 1994. The manga has run for over 1,100 chapters and remains ongoing, making it one of the longest-running manga series in publication history.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many million copies of Detective Conan (Case Closed) have been sold globally?
facts.explanation: Detective Conan has sold over 270 million copies worldwide, placing it among the best-selling manga series of all time alongside One Piece and Golgo 13. The TMS Entertainment anime adaptation has aired over 1,100 episodes since 1996.

facts.quizQuestion: Which manga creator is responsible for both Yu Yu Hakusho and Hunter x Hunter?
facts.explanation: Yoshihiro Togashi created both Yu Yu Hakusho (1990-1994) and Hunter x Hunter (1998-present), making him one of the most celebrated shonen manga authors. Yu Yu Hakusho ran for 175 chapters in Weekly Shonen Jump and was adapted into a 112-episode anime by Pierrot.

facts.quizQuestion: Which creator, also known for One Punch Man, wrote and drew Mob Psycho 100?
facts.explanation: ONE created Mob Psycho 100 as a webcomic starting in 2012, running it alongside One Punch Man. Bones produced two seasons of the anime (2016 and 2019) plus a third season in 2022, all of which were praised for their innovative animation style depicting psychic powers.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the real name of the psychically overpowered middle school student nicknamed 'Mob' in Mob Psycho 100?
facts.explanation: Shigeo Kageyama, nicknamed Mob because of his plain appearance, is an extremely powerful esper who tries to suppress his emotions to avoid losing control of his psychic powers. His mentor is the fraudulent psychic Arataka Reigen, who forms the heart of the series' comedic and emotional core.

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the Fairy Tail manga, which ran in Weekly Shonen Magazine from 2006 to 2017?
facts.explanation: Hiro Mashima created Fairy Tail, which ran for 545 chapters in Kodansha's Weekly Shonen Magazine from 2006 to 2017. The anime adaptation was produced by A-1 Pictures and later Satelight, totaling over 300 episodes. Mashima is also known for creating Rave Master and Edens Zero.

facts.quizQuestion: In which magazine was the Fairy Tail manga serialized by publisher Kodansha?
facts.explanation: Fairy Tail was serialized in Kodansha's Weekly Shonen Magazine, a rival to Shueisha's Weekly Shonen Jump. While Jump is the more globally famous publication, Magazine has a long history of hit series including initial serializations of Hajime no Ippo, Negima, and Elfen Lied.
